sentences.text: Well, we had in the ghetto, we had so-called, a Judenalteste.
His name was [?
Rumkowski, ?] And he was leading the ghetto.
Before the war he was a president of an orphaned home.
And the first people who, every month in the ghetto, they had to deport so many and so many people.
So the first, he gave away his children.
So he deported all the young children.
We never knew in ghetto what was happening in 24 hours.
We all could be gone.
We lived just from day to day.
We-- in the court I was living.
They was-- they maybe with 50 tenants.
Everybody was very cramped in one room.
The winters were very cold.
We just lived.
We were heating the houses to cook the smaller rations we got, what we could get a hold-- boards from the floors, boards from whenever you could get a piece of wood.
By the end of the ghetto, we had no one-- not even one board on the floor, because we used it for fuel.
We went to the kitchen.
We got one ration of soup every single day.
Who was lucky got a potato, and who came the last didn't get any, got just a little bit of water.
They had commune kitchens.
We didn't have a kitchen.
They had commune kitchen, and we were getting a ration every day.
Only the people who worked, but the people who didn't work didn't get anything.
The daily food consist of a little soup and one slice of bread.
We would go to the kitchen with a little-- whatever we had, the utensil.
And we took the soup back home, or we took it back to the factory.
Every day we got that soup.
And I knew-- going back in the beginning of the ghetto-- I knew that my mother was home.
So I took the soup every other day and gave it to her.
So I ate like every other day.
Life in ghetto, in the court, we were sitting together.
And we were just hoping maybe one day this will end.
All the people from the court, what, we were maybe 50 tenants, a lot of young people.
The ghetto was wired with barbed wires.
Like every five meter was a SS.
Like you couldn't run way.
There was no way to run, because the Polacks would never house us.
They were-- they were-- right away they were the biggest traitors.
Nobody would help us.
[SIGHS] And right in the beginning of the ghetto, we had a school, like 1941.
But then they took the school away.
Like we would-- there were a lot of children in the ghetto.
There were a lot of people which lived there before the war.
Like a regular school, we were reading to the younger children.
In Polish.
Everything was in Polish.
The ghetto life-- we could speak to the people in the court.
There was no way after work to go out.
We could not walk.
So we walked as young people between the buildings.
But as the ghetto got-- as every single month, they deported so many and so many people.
Whoever was lucky could stay.
I was in hiding for months and months.
So they did not deport me.
No, if at working they deported people.
I did not go to work.
Nothing.
Somehow, whatever we had.
We ate whatever we could get a hold of it.
We were so hungry for six years we could eat a tree.
We could eat a board, we were so hungry.
And let's see.
I'm going back here.
We went, we were-- when they had every month, they had to give, the alteste was [?
Rumkowski-- ?] he had to give so many people to send to Auschwitz to exterminate.
So every month, the ghetto was getting [? looser ?] and [? looser ?] and [? looser. ?] And the houses were getting emptier and emptier.
For fuel, we went to the all-- to the empty homes.
We tore out the boards to heat.
Was very-- Poland was very, very cold.
It was like sometimes 10 below zero.
[SIGHS]
My other-- 1942, they had the biggest roundup in ghetto.
And they took all the young people and all the old people.
At that time, it was about 10 days.
The Nazis, the Germans came in with the big trucks.
And they round up the people and took them to extermination camps.
At that time, my sister was at that time 12 years old.
She was beautiful.
She was blonde with blue eyes.
Very thin, because when the war start, she was about nine years old.
And she always said to me, I'm going to be taking away.
I'm going to be taking away.
I said, no.
You're going to be where I am.
But she wasn't so lucky.
At one of the round ups, they took her away.
And they put her on a big, big truck with a lot of young people.
And they took them to a city.
The name was Chelmno.
And right away they all were gassed.
They took all the young children.
I remember they tore out children from the mother's hands.
Like infants, a year old, a half year old.
In the ghetto-- in the court where I lived, it was a very big court.
The Nazis came upstairs, and they threw the children down from three, four floors, right on the truck.
And they put-- took all the people right away to Chelmno, and they were gassed.
[SOBBING] After they took my sister away--
Well, we were hoping that she was going someplace where to survive.
But we found this out later, about a year later, after they took my sister away.
She was beautiful.
She was very bright.
She was only 12 years old.
She was too young to leave.
At that time, she spoke-- she was going to a private Talmud school, and she spoke a beautiful Hebrew.
And she always said, look, I'm so little.
I'm so thin.
There was no food.
She was too young to work.
So I always shared with her.
Whatever I got, I brought home and shared.
She-- she survived till 1942.
At that roundup, they took her away.
And I was sitting at the window numb, hoping that I'm going to see her again.
But I have never, never saw her again.
They took them all to Chelmno.
It was a place not too far from Lodz.
They all were gassed.
No.
One sister survived with me, which is alive now.
And she lives in Israel.
She's two years younger than I am.
She was left with me.
She was at the time about 15.
Yes, she was 15 years old at the time.
I was 16.
And she was-- No, this was later.
This was-- the roundup was in 1942.
I was about 15, and she was 14.
And she was left.
She was working in the factory.
Everybody had to work.
Who didn't work did not get a ration.
You just had to work.
If you didn't work, you were dead.
You had absolutely nothing.
And there was-- the ghetto was very badly managed.
They had no support.
They was-- the food was rotten before it was passed to the people.
Whoever had somebody from the leaders, they got a little bit more.
But whoever didn't knew anybody, with no luck, got nothing.
We just had to live for what we got.
We were- we were given a home of people which already were sent away to extermination camps.
So everything was left.
And a little-- like in one room, maybe 10 people slept.
And every day, it was getting [? looser ?] and [? looser. ?] So we had more room and more room, because people died.
There was terrible typhus going in ghetto on.
It-- were terrible diseases, so people were dying.
I remember in ghetto, [SIGHS] going back when my mother died.
This was 1942.
So many people were dying that they did not even have enough manpower to bury the people.
It was-- it was typhus going.
In fact, I was very lucky.
I have, once they tested me a while back.
I have typhus, but it's not active.
I was with so many sick people in the ghetto.
We made a lot of friendship in the court we lived.
And I still have them up till now.
We were young, and a few hours we could spend together after work.
So we had very strong friendships.
We knew when it was the holiday.
And we talked.
We sat around in the court.
And we were just hoping for a better tomorrow.
We just was talking-- we just were talking, maybe this will end one day.
Maybe we have a better tomorrow.
Every day we didn't know what's going to be tomorrow.
We could have been round up and sent away.
Of course we talked about the past.
We were sharing about the past.
It-- all we were wanting is something to eat.
We could've bit on a stone, we were so hungry.
We had nothing, absolutely nothing to eat.
I can't even understand how we survived.
I guess if you are young, you survive.
People were dying like flies every day in ghetto.
And whatever we could get a hold of it, we ate.
I guess not.
If I was sick I wouldn't live.
I was never sick.
I saw everybody around me dying.
I was staying with my mother.
I remember my mother and father, they died of starvation in ghetto in 1942.
I was sleeping with the corpses in the house, [SOBBING] for about maybe a week till they could take them away.
We lost all our feelings.
We didn't feel at anything.
It was so terrible.
People were walking in the street and dying like flies.
It was a big epidemic.
Poland had very terrible winters.
It was very cold.
No fuel.
Nothing.
And we had no way to know how the other part of the building lives.
There was no way.
We were always watched, not to talk to anybody, not to see anybody.
We're going to work and coming home, going to work and coming home.
At times it was so cold that we paired up six people in bed to warm our bodies together.
Then the ghetto was getting [? looser ?] and [? looser. ?] So many people died.
Every few weeks they had the roundups in ghetto and took away people.
One day [SOBBING] in 1944, they had a great big roundup in ghetto.
The German trucks came in with dogs, and they round up the ghetto.
They went from room to room.
[SOBBING] They threw little children from the windows on the trucks to-- we didn't know where they were going, to extermination camps.
I was-- we saw what was going on, so I went with my sister in hiding.
I didn't live already in the house I lived before.
It got so [? loose. ?] So they closed one part, and I went to the other part of the bridge to live with my cousin.
We were hiding in a basement for week-- for weeks, day after day, with very, very little food.
Whatever we could get a hold of it from the empty homes-- during the night we searched-- and whatever we could find, we ate.
In this basement, we had [SOBBING] at the beginning, we had maybe 10, 12 people.
There was a lady with a baby.
And the baby start to cry, and they found us.
Actually, it were not basement.
It was like it a little trap.
And on the trap, we put a table on.
And then the Germans found us.
They didn't find me and my sister.
And I was still there, but they took out the child and the mother and the father.
And they went right away to-- on the truck [BACKROUND NOISE] to Chelmno, to extermination camp.
We found this out later.
We didn't know.
I was not.
I was still covered up.
We were there for weeks hiding.
And we went at night out for a little air.
And went to the empty homes, which people were taken away, to find something to eat.
This basement, this hiding place was like was in-- back in Poland, life was very primitive.
There was no running water in my days.
There was electricity.
I remember when I was little girl, maybe 12 years old, the first electricity.
And the basement was like a trap in the-- in the floor, and it was covered.
And we cooled our food there, it shouldn't spoil-- in there where we were sitting.
No window, nothing.
It was just like a hole in the ground, and we covered it up.
And that's how we were there.
It was maybe big like-- how big?
Maybe five by five.
Like a room-- a small room.
Like a small, and we were, a lot of people choked.
It was so crowded.
But it was getting [? looser ?] and [? looser ?] as they took the people out.
I just can't understand how we survived.
It is really beyond belief as I think back.
But I guess if you're young, you don't think.
I always had my sister with me.
At times she said, leave me.
At night, she was so weak and so hungry.
So I pulled her by the hair to get out for air at night.
But we stayed there for weeks and weeks, till one day, we could-- we could hear every day steps from the soldiers on the floor.
We had to be so quiet.
You didn't hear a peep.
There were no children anymore in the ghetto.
There with no sick people anymore.
There were no older people.
Just a few young ones survived.
So we were there hiding till about 1944, till September.
Then we saw that it got very quiet.
No more steps.
We got out from the hiding place.
I was with my sister and with my cousin, Masza, which she survived.
She lives in Chicago now.
And then we said, what do we do now?
It was a ghost town.
The ghetto was empty.
Everybody was taken away.
So we volunteered, and we went to the station.
We thought that we are going to a better place.
So they took us to Auschwitz.
OK.
I need to ask you.
Who-- who-- you volunteered to whom?
We just left.
We volunteered to go someplace to find [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some food.
No.
It was different.
There were loud speakers at the end of the ghetto.
And the Germans said, whoever is alive, and whoever is in hiding, should get out and go to the station, that we are go to a working camp.
We we going to be together with families, and we're going to have enough to eat.
So we did.
By the Germans.
So it was nothing to lose.
There was a ghost town.
There was nothing to eat.
So I took my sister and my cousin, and we went to the station, which was maybe two miles.
And we went on the train going to-- we didn't know where we was going-- but they took us to Auschwitz.
Can you describe that-- that experience a little bit more?
I was so numb that I don't even know.
I was all numb.
I lost everybody.
I was left with my younger sister, which was so weak she could hardly walk.
There were a lot of people going to the station.
They loaded us on trains, on cattle trains with no windows.
We didn't even had space to sit.
We were standing.
People were dying like flies.
It was getting [? looser ?] and [? looser. ?] On the way, they were throwing corpses [SOBBING] down.
And [SIGHS] all of a sudden, we see gates.
They took us, then we found out that we are in a big concentration camp, in Auschwitz.
For days.
And it was, from Lodz to Auschwitz was very close.
It's maybe a two or three hours.
But they confused us.
And they had us on the train with absolutely no food, very little food for days.
I don't remember how many days we were there.
Just a little food.
If not, we wouldn't survive.
Just a little food.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I arrived to Auschwitz.
When we arrived to Auschwitz, we got off from the train, and the German soldier made a selection.
I arrived with my sister, and they said, here, here, here, here.
There were a lot of people.
There were people with children.
There were men and women.
And they selected the people.
Children separate.
All the people separate.
Younger people separate.
Women separate.
Men separate.
[SIGHS] We were so confused that it was hard to remember.
So I was together, lucky, with my sister.
So they took us to a bath.
We didn't know what was going on.
But lucky, later I realized that this was not the gas chamber, that this was a regular bath.
They took us to the bath.
They stripped us whatever we had.
We had little bit of jewelry.
They took our clothes off.
They cut us-- our hair flat-- my sister and I, and my cousin.
And then we went into that shower so-called, from one door.
And we left all naked through another door.
They gave us some clothes.
They gave me-- I was tall-- a very short dress.
The short ones they gave a long dress, whatever.
And I stood, and I looked at my sister.
I didn't recognize her.
She didn't recognize me.
All-- we were all, our hair was shaved off.
We were stripped of our clothes.
We just were so confused.
We didn't know what was going on.
Then we walked into a big, big camp with barb wires, with a lot of people.
I saw the wires, people were committing suicides.
People were dying.
They were children there.
Then we were standing there, and it was so cold.
It was already September.
So we did-- made a circle to warm our bodies.
[SIGHS] And then--
Me, and my cousin, and my sister, and a lot of other people.
We were-- day after day, it was a selection.
They selected the young people, the children, the men separate.
So it was a group of people.
Then they gave us a barrack near the gas chamber, in Auschwitz, barrack 20.
And the girl said, the SS said, you don't have to worry about your life.
So we understood that our life is over.
We went to the barrack, [SIGHS] and we stayed there for about three days.
The conditions were terrible.
People were dying every single day.
People were walking and dying.
There were so many people.
No food whatsoever.
Just a little bit to survive.
Then-- [SIGHS]
The barrack was like a barn with wood-- with wooden bridges, just straw with nothing.
I remember one incident at the barrack.
We were laying there.
A lady came from the ghetto in her nine month pregnant.
And she delivered a child.
She was crying, and she was bleeding.
She-- now I can recollect this.
She was bleeding and hemorrhaging terribly, so she called her husband.
I don't remember his name.
And she said, I love you.
I love you.
Where are you?
And she delivered the child.
[SIGHS] So the SS came over.
She wrapped the child in a newspaper and took it away.
I don't know what happened to it.
There were so many people in the barracks that there was no room even to stand out.
People were dying like flies.
It was getting [? looser ?] and [? looser. ?] We got [SIGHS] very, very little food.
Every day people were taken out to the crematorium.
I still was holding onto my sister, was with my sister all the time.
Somehow, they took 200 girls out from the barrack.
They still thought that we are healthy and strong enough to work.
So they took us out on appell.
They stripped our clothes.
And [SIGHS] somebody came, like a businessman, and selected us to take to work.
So they selected about 200 girls from the Lodz ghetto, and we were sent in to a concentration camp to Bad Kudowa, Sackisch.
This was the German and Polish border.
And I went there.
How did you get there?
I went there by train, with a few of the girls I knew from the ghetto.
There was one girl-- which is my very close friend-- her name is Bella, and her sister.
We knew them from ghetto.
And it was my sister, another girl, which her name is Frieda Goodrich.
She lives, she survived.
And she lives now in the Valley.
Another few people which live now in Israel.
And again they went through a big selections.
And I guess I was still young and pretty healthy looking, so they took us to a concentration camp, to Bad Kudowa, Sackisch.
It was on the border of Germany and Czechoslovakia.
We got there by train.
When we arrived to that camp, it was-- [SIGHS] They told us, you don't have to worry about your life.
So we knew that we don't have to worry about our life, that we won't live too long.
We got to the camp.
There were two hundred girls.
And they set us up in barracks.
We all were young.
And we had German SS watching over us.
This was in Czechoslovakia, in Bad Kudowa.
Always held on to my sister.
She was with me.
Was lucky.
And they sent us up in the barracks, and then they sent us to work to ammunition plant.
It was a plant.
It looked like mountains.
We were 200 girls.
And the plant they were working.
They were producing the [INAUDIBLE].
I was quite intelligent.
And I knew the German language very well, because I went to a very good school in Poland.
So sort of they picked me I should be an interpreter.
So they gave me a little bit of extra bread.
And I was an interpreter for a few months.
In fact, I recall one incident that the German said, I just can't understand what they did [SOBBING] with you.
You're so intelligent.
What did they do to you?
Remember when we came to the factory at concentration camp, they had big signs on the walls.
And they said don't speak with those girls.
They whores.
They came from a prison.
That's why they came to concentration camp.
But some--
